story.title: Dr. Chen's Guide to Wind Chill
story.text:
Hello! I am Dr. Sarah Chen. I am a research scientist. At my lab, we study complex systems to see how things connect.

Let's solve a puzzle. Why does a windy day feel colder than a calm day? The air temperature is the same, but you feel much colder. Your body warms up a thin layer of air right next to your skin. This is your thermal boundary layer. It acts like a tiny, invisible blanket that keeps you warm.

But strong wind blows this warm layer away. This process is called convection. When the warm air leaves, your skin is exposed to the colder air again. This leads to convective heat loss. The faster the wind blows, the faster your body loses heat. Your skin temperature drops. This makes your brain feel a greater sense of cold. We call this feeling the wind chill. It is the perceived temperature.

So, the wind does not change the air temperature. Instead, it changes how quickly your body loses heat to the air.
think_abouts: What does wind do to the warm layer of air around your body?
Is 'wind chill' the actual temperature of the air?
story.go_deeper: How might animals with thick fur or feathers be protected from convective heat loss?

margin_defs.definition: Heat moving with the flow of air or liquid.
A thin layer of warm air right next to your skin.
How cold your body feels because of the wind.

check_prompts: True or False: Wind makes the air colder.

vocabulary.definition: The transfer of heat by the movement of a fluid, like air or water.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A fan uses convection to move cool air around a room.

vocabulary.definition: A measure of how cold it feels on the skin when wind is combined with air temperature.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The wind chill made the 30-degree day feel like it was 15 degrees.

vocabulary.definition: A very thin layer of still, warm air that forms on the surface of your skin.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Your jacket helps protect your thermal boundary layer from the wind.

vocabulary.definition: The process of losing heat when a fluid, like wind, moves across a surface.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Getting out of a pool on a windy day causes rapid convective heat loss.

vocabulary.definition: The temperature that your body feels, which can be different from the measured air temperature.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Because of the high humidity, the perceived temperature was hotter than the thermometer showed.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Chen describes the thermal boundary layer as an 'invisible blanket.' What are some other things that act like this layer to keep objects warm?
discussion_cards.frame: Something that acts like a thermal boundary layer is ___ because it ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: How could Dr. Chen design an experiment to test which materials are best at stopping convective heat loss?
discussion_cards.frame: To test different materials, Dr. Chen could start by ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
